1: The two baskets of figs before the temple: a vision given after Jehoiachin's deportation (597 BCE). The temple setting of the vision — figs placed before the LORD as offerings — makes the interpretation covenantal: YHWH is evaluating the quality of what is brought before him. Jesus's parable of the fig tree (Luke 13:6-9) and the cursed fig tree (Matthew 21:18-22; Mark 11:12-14) both engage this fig-as-covenant-symbol tradition. The temple and the fig tree are interconnected in Jesus's final week.
2: Very good figs like first-ripe figs (bikkurot) — the highest quality, the first of the harvest, most prized — versus figs so rotten they cannot be eaten. The contrast is absolute: no middle ground, no 'acceptable but not great.' Jesus's parable of the wheat and tares (Matthew 13:24-30, 36-43) has the same structure: two categories, same apparent field, final sorting. The good figs represent the exiles YHWH is about to do good to; the bad figs those who remain in Jerusalem under Zedekiah.
3: YHWH asks Jeremiah what he sees. The prophetic dialogue method — YHWH invites the prophet's observation before giving the interpretation. Jesus frequently asks his disciples what they see or perceive (Matthew 16:13-15: 'Who do people say the Son of Man is?'; Matthew 13:51: 'Have you understood all these things?'). The Socratic method of prophetic pedagogy: the observation precedes the revelation.
4: The word of the LORD coming — the transition from observation to interpretation. Jesus's teaching moves between observation (looking at the birds, the lilies, the fig tree) and interpretation (the kingdom of God is like this). The rhythm of see-then-interpret is the educational method of the prophetic tradition into which Jesus inserts himself as the definitive teacher (Matthew 7:28-29).
5: 'Like these good figs, I will look favorably on the exiles of Judah whom I sent away from this place to the land of the Babylonians.' YHWH regards the exile as a divine sending for good, not merely a judgment. Jesus's understanding of his own 'sending' into the world has the same logic: he is sent by the Father (John 3:17) for the good of those he goes to, not merely to condemn. The exiled people in Babylon, apparently abandoned, are being specially cared for.
6: 'I will keep my eyes on them for good, and I will bring them back to this land. I will build them and not tear down, plant them and not uproot.' The four reversal pairs — build/tear down, plant/uproot — reverse the charter of Jeremiah 1:10 ('to tear down and to plant, to destroy and to build'). Jesus's ministry is the new-covenant version: 'I will build my church, and the gates of Hades will not overcome it' (Matthew 16:18). The one who tears down the old structures builds the new one that cannot be pulled down.
7: 'I will give them a heart to know me, that I am the LORD. They will be my people and I will be their God, for they will return to me with all their heart.' The proto-new-covenant verse of Jeremiah 24: the lev lada-at oti (heart to know me) is the forerunner of Jeremiah 31:33-34 ('I will put my law in their minds and write it on their hearts... they will all know me'). Jesus gives this heart through the Spirit: 'the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in my name, will teach you all things' (John 14:26); 'they will all be taught by God' (John 6:45 citing Isaiah 54:13). The heart-to-know-YHWH that exile could not produce, the Spirit provides through Christ.
8: 'But as for the bad figs ... Zedekiah king of Judah, his officials, the survivors in Jerusalem, and those who remain in Egypt.' The category of those who did not go into exile, who resisted YHWH's purposes by staying. Jesus's warning: 'whoever finds their life will lose it, and whoever loses their life for my sake will find it' (Matthew 10:39). The counterintuitive reversal: those who held onto their position in Jerusalem (the 'good' outcome) are the bad figs; those who lost everything to exile are the good figs. Surrender to YHWH's purposes, even painful ones, is the path of covenant.
9: The bad figs become a horror to all kingdoms — reproach, byword, taunt, curse everywhere YHWH drives them. The covenant-curse catalog (Deuteronomy 28:37: 'you will become a thing of horror, a byword and an object of ridicule among all the peoples where the LORD will drive you'). Jesus bears all these titles at the cross (Matthew 27:39-44: 'those who passed by hurled insults at him... the robbers who were crucified with him also heaped insults on him'). He takes the position of the bad-fig curse upon himself so that those who trust him become the good figs — transformed.
10: 'And I will send the sword, famine, and pestilence against them until they are completely destroyed from the land.' The triple judgment formula — sword, famine, pestilence — is the standard covenant-curse triad (Leviticus 26:25-26; Deuteronomy 28:22-25; Jeremiah 14:12; Jeremiah 21:7). Jesus predicts the same triple judgment coming on Jerusalem in his generation (Matthew 24:6-7: wars, famines, earthquakes as 'the beginning of birth pangs'). The complete removal from the land is the ultimate covenant consequence — which he enters and absorbs so that a new community can be grafted in.
